description: DocuSync standard retrieval benchmark — 30 QA pairs across 5 categories. Self-contained: all test documents are embedded as text so the benchmark runs without any external downloads.

categories.name: Exact Lookup
categories.description: Course codes, professor names, specific identifiers

categories.name: Policy Retrieval
categories.description: Attendance, grading, late work, academic integrity

categories.name: Semantic Concept
categories.description: Topic-level queries without exact term overlap

categories.name: Cross-Document
categories.description: Queries where answer spans multiple documents

test_documents.content:
CS 301 — Data Structures and Algorithms
Instructor: Prof. Sarah Smith | Spring 2024 | MWF 10:00–10:50 AM | Room: CS 205

Course Description
This course covers fundamental data structures including arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, heaps, graphs, and hash tables. Algorithm design techniques including recursion, divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, and greedy algorithms are studied. Time complexity analysis using Big-O notation is a core component.

Prerequisites: CS 101 (Introduction to Programming) with a grade of C or higher.

Required Textbook: Introduction to Algorithms, 4th Edition, Cormen et al. (CLRS), ISBN 978-0262046305
Programming Language: Python 3.10 or higher. All assignments submitted via GitHub.

Grading
Homework Assignments (6 total): 30%
Midterm Exam: 25%
Final Exam: 30%
Lab Participation: 15%

Attendance Policy
Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a 5% deduction per absence from the final grade. Attendance is tracked using iClicker.

Late Work Policy
Late assignments receive a 10% penalty per day. No submissions accepted after 5 days past the deadline. Extensions require written approval from Prof. Smith at least 48 hours in advance.

Academic Integrity
All work must be your own. Use of generative AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, etc.) for assignment solutions is prohibited. Violations will result in an automatic F for the course.

Office Hours: Monday and Wednesday 2:00–4:00 PM, CS Building Room 312. Additional hours by appointment via email: sarah.smith@university.edu

MATH 401 — Linear Algebra
Instructor: Prof. James Johnson | Fall 2023 | TR 1:00–2:15 PM | Room: Math 110

Course Description
Advanced treatment of linear algebra. Topics include vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, inner product spaces, orthogonality, singular value decomposition (SVD), and applications to data science and machine learning.

Prerequisites: MATH 201 (Multivariable Calculus) and MATH 220 (Discrete Mathematics).

Required Software: MATLAB R2023a. Student licenses available via university IT portal. All homework submitted as MATLAB live scripts (.mlx).

Grading
Problem Sets (8 total): 40%
Midterm 1: 15%
Midterm 2: 15%
Final Exam: 30%

Attendance Policy
No formal attendance requirement. Lecture slides posted on Canvas within 24 hours of each class.

Late Work Policy
No late submissions accepted under any circumstances. Problem sets are due at 11:59 PM on the listed date. Submit early if you anticipate conflicts.

Exam Policy
All exams are closed-book. A one-page formula sheet (both sides, handwritten) is permitted for each exam. Calculators are not allowed.

Office Hours: Tuesday and Thursday 3:00–5:00 PM, Math Building Room 220.

BIO 151 — Introduction to Cell Biology
Instructor: Prof. Michelle Lee | Spring 2024 | MWF 11:00–11:50 AM | Room: Life Sciences 301

Course Description
Foundations of cell biology including cell structure and function, membrane transport, cellular respiration, photosynthesis, the cell cycle, mitosis, meiosis, DNA replication, transcription, and translation. Laboratory sections meet separately (2 hrs/week).

Prerequisites: CHEM 101 (General Chemistry I) or concurrent enrollment.

Required Textbook: Molecular Biology of the Cell, 7th Edition, Alberts et al. ISBN 978-0393884821.

Grading
Exams (3 midterms, no drops): 45%
Final Exam: 25%
Lab Reports (6 total): 20%
Group Project — Cell Research Presentation: 10%

Group Project
Students will form groups of 3–4 and present a 15-minute research presentation on a cell biology topic of their choice. Topics must be approved by Week 4. Each group member must speak for at least 3 minutes. Peer evaluation counts for 20% of the project grade.

Late Work Policy
Lab reports: 10% deduction per day late. Group presentations cannot be rescheduled except for university-excused absences.

Office Hours: Monday and Friday 1:00–3:00 PM, Life Sciences Building Room 115. Zoom link available on Canvas.

ECE 452 — Digital Signal Processing
Instructor: Prof. Wei Chen | Fall 2023 | TR 9:30–10:45 AM | Room: Engineering 408

Course Description
Discrete-time signals and systems, the Z-transform, DFT and FFT algorithms, FIR and IIR digital filter design, quantization effects, multirate signal processing, and introduction to adaptive filtering. MATLAB is used throughout for design and simulation.

Prerequisites: ECE 301 (Signals and Systems) and MATH 401 (Linear Algebra) or concurrent enrollment.

Required Software: MATLAB with the Signal Processing Toolbox. Python with SciPy is accepted as an alternative.

Grading
Homework (10 assignments): 35%
Midterm Exam: 25%
Final Exam: 30%
Final Project (individual, MATLAB/Python implementation): 10%

Attendance Policy
Attendance is strongly encouraged but not graded. Lecture recordings will not be provided; missing class is at your own risk.

Late Work Policy
Homework late penalty: 20% per day, maximum 3 days. After 3 days, assignment receives 0.

Safety
All lab work requires closed-toe shoes and safety glasses. Students violating lab safety rules will be asked to leave.

Final Exam: December 14, 2023, 8:00–10:00 AM, Engineering 408.

ECON 201 — Principles of Microeconomics
Instructor: Prof. Carlos Garcia | Spring 2024 | MWF 9:00–9:50 AM | Room: Social Sciences 120

Course Description
Introduction to microeconomic theory: supply and demand, consumer and producer theory, market structures (perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly), game theory basics, externalities, and public goods. Policy applications include minimum wage, price controls, taxation, and environmental regulation.

Prerequisites: None. Calculus is helpful but not required.

Required Textbook: Principles of Economics, 9th Edition, Mankiw.
Calculator: A basic scientific calculator is permitted on exams; smartphones are not.

Grading
Quizzes (weekly, best 10 of 12): 20%
Midterm Exam 1: 20%
Midterm Exam 2: 20%
Final Exam: 30%
Class Participation: 10%

Attendance Policy
Attendance is not formally tracked. However, in-class participation (clicker questions, discussions) counts for 10% of your grade and cannot be made up.

Late Work Policy
No late quizzes accepted. Quiz answers are posted immediately after class ends.

Office Hours: MWF 10:00–11:00 AM and by appointment. Room: Social Sciences 215.

CS 568 — Machine Learning
Instructor: Prof. Robert Williams | Fall 2023 | TR 2:00–3:15 PM | Room: CS 310

Course Description
Core machine learning algorithms and their theoretical foundations. Topics include supervised learning (linear regression, logistic regression, SVMs, decision trees, ensemble methods), unsupervised learning (k-means, PCA, autoencoders), neural networks and deep learning (feedforward, CNN, RNN, Transformers), reinforcement learning basics, and practical ML engineering (data pipelines, feature engineering, model evaluation, hyperparameter tuning).

Prerequisites: CS 301 (Data Structures), MATH 401 (Linear Algebra), STAT 302 (Probability and Statistics). Python proficiency required.

Required Tools: Python 3.10+, PyTorch 2.0+, scikit-learn, pandas, NumPy. All assignments run on university GPU cluster (instructions on Canvas).

Grading
Programming Assignments (5 total): 40%
Midterm Exam: 20%
Final Project (team of 2, original ML application): 25%
Paper Reviews (4 total, 2 pages each): 15%

Final Project
Teams of 2 will build an original machine learning application and present results in a 12-minute poster session during finals week. A 6-page report in NeurIPS format is required. Dataset and code must be released on GitHub.

Late Work Policy
Programming assignments: 20% deduction per day, no submissions beyond 3 days. Paper reviews: 0 if late. Final project: deadline is firm.

Office Hours: Prof. Williams — Tuesday 4:00–6:00 PM, CS 315. TAs hold office hours listed on Canvas.

Email: r.williams@university.edu | Response within 48 hours.

CHEM 122 — Organic Chemistry I
Instructor: Prof. Patricia Brown | Spring 2024 | MWF 8:00–8:50 AM | Room: Chemistry 201

Course Description
Structure, bonding, and reactivity of organic compounds. Topics include nomenclature, stereochemistry, substitution and elimination reactions, addition reactions to alkenes and alkynes, carbonyl chemistry, and spectroscopic methods (IR, NMR, MS).

Prerequisites: CHEM 101 and CHEM 102 (General Chemistry I and II) with grades of C or higher.

Required Lab: CHEM 122L (co-requisite). Lab safety certification must be completed before Week 1.

Required Textbook: Organic Chemistry, 10th Edition, Wade & Simek. ISBN 978-0134161846.

Grading
Lecture Exams (3): 45%
Final Exam: 30%
Lab Grade (from CHEM 122L): 20%
Online Homework (Mastering Chemistry): 5%

Lab Safety
Lab coats, safety goggles, and closed-toe shoes are mandatory at all times in the laboratory. Students without proper safety equipment will not be allowed to participate and will receive a zero for that lab. Students must complete the safety certification quiz with a score of 100% before attending their first lab session.

Attendance Policy
Lab attendance is mandatory. Missing more than 2 lab sessions results in automatic course failure regardless of lecture performance.

Office Hours: Monday 9:00–11:00 AM and Wednesday 1:00–3:00 PM, Chemistry Building Room 310.

PSYCH 301 — Research Methods in Psychology
Instructor: Prof. Angela Davis | Fall 2023 | TR 11:00 AM–12:15 PM | Room: Tobin 220

Course Description
Scientific methods in psychological research. Topics include experimental design, observational methods, survey methodology, reliability and validity, IRB ethics, data collection, statistical analysis using SPSS and R, and scientific writing in APA 7th edition format.

Prerequisites: PSYCH 101 and STAT 101 (Introduction to Statistics).

Required Software: R and RStudio (free), SPSS (university license available). All reports must be written in APA 7th edition format.

Grading
Research Proposal (individual): 15%
Lab Reports (4 total): 40%
Final Research Paper (APA format, 8–10 pages): 25%
Class Participation and Peer Review: 10%
Final Exam: 10%

Writing Requirement
This is a writing-intensive course. All written work must follow APA 7th edition guidelines. The Writing Center offers free consultations; students are strongly encouraged to use this resource before submitting major assignments.

Ethics
All research involving human subjects must be approved by the IRB (Institutional Review Board). Students may not collect data from real participants without prior written IRB approval.

Late Work Policy
Late papers receive a 5-point deduction per day (out of 100). Work more than 7 days late receives a 0.

Office Hours: Tuesday and Thursday 1:00–3:00 PM, Tobin Hall Room 310.

MGMT 450 — Strategic Management
Instructor: Prof. Thomas Wilson | Spring 2024 | TR 3:30–4:45 PM | Room: Isenberg 114

Course Description
Capstone course in business strategy. Topics include competitive analysis (Porter's Five Forces, SWOT), resource-based view, corporate strategy (vertical integration, diversification), international strategy, strategic alliances, mergers and acquisitions, and strategy implementation. Heavy use of real-world case studies from Harvard Business Review and MIT Sloan.

Prerequisites: MGMT 300 (Principles of Management), FIN 301 (Corporate Finance), MKTG 301 (Marketing Management). Open to seniors and juniors with department permission.

Case Studies
Approximately 3 case studies per week will be assigned. All students must read cases before class. Cold-calling is used extensively. A written case analysis (1 page, single-spaced) is due for 8 of the assigned cases.

Grading
Case Analyses (8 submissions): 25%
Midterm Exam: 20%
Group Project — Strategic Consulting Report: 30%
Final Exam: 15%
Class Participation: 10%

Group Project
Groups of 4–5 students will select a real company, conduct a full strategic analysis, and present recommendations to the class in a 20-minute presentation. A 15-page written report is required. Industry sponsor presentations are possible for top groups.

Attendance and Participation
Participation is graded. Missing more than 2 classes without prior notice will affect your participation grade significantly. The case discussion format requires preparation; unprepared students may be asked to leave.

Office Hours: Tuesday 5:30–7:00 PM, Isenberg 225. Prof. Wilson is also reachable via LinkedIn.

STAT 302 — Probability and Statistics
Instructor: Prof. Elena Martinez | Fall 2023 | MWF 2:00–2:50 PM | Room: Math 215

Course Description
Mathematical foundations of probability and statistics. Topics include sample spaces, probability axioms, conditional probability, Bayes theorem, discrete and continuous random variables (Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential, Gamma distributions), expectation and variance, moment generating functions, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, linear regression, and introduction to Bayesian inference.

Prerequisites: MATH 201 (Multivariable Calculus). Familiarity with basic programming in R or Python is helpful.

Required Software: R and RStudio. Homework submitted as R Markdown (.Rmd) files knitted to PDF.

Grading
Homework (10 assignments): 35%
Midterm 1 (Chapters 1–5): 20%
Midterm 2 (Chapters 6–10): 20%
Final Exam (comprehensive): 25%

Attendance Policy
Attendance is not required but strongly recommended. The pace is fast; falling behind is difficult to recover from.

Late Work Policy
Homework accepted up to 48 hours late with a 20% penalty. After 48 hours, 0 points.

Collaboration Policy
Discussion with classmates is encouraged. However, all written solutions must be your own. Submitting identical solutions is academic dishonesty.

Final Exam: December 12, 2023, 3:30–5:30 PM, Math 215. Bring student ID.
Office Hours: Monday and Wednesday 3:00–5:00 PM, Math Building Room 118.

queries.query: CS 568 machine learning course
queries.notes: Exact course code match — BM25 should dominate

queries.query: Prof. Sarah Smith office hours
queries.notes: Exact name lookup

queries.query: MATH 401 final exam date
queries.notes: Course code + query type

queries.query: ECE 452 final exam December 2023
queries.notes: Date + course code — both BM25 and semantic should find this

queries.query: CS 301 prerequisites
queries.notes: Course code + field name

queries.query: Prof. Elena Martinez STAT 302
queries.notes: Double exact match — professor name + course code

queries.query: attendance policy penalty absences
queries.notes: Policy keyword query — multiple docs have attendance policies

queries.query: late submission penalty per day
queries.notes: 4 documents have explicit per-day late penalties

queries.query: no late submissions accepted
queries.notes: Exact policy phrasing — BM25 should rank these high

queries.query: academic integrity AI tools prohibited generative
queries.notes: Only CS 301 explicitly mentions AI tool prohibition

queries.query: lab safety equipment requirements
queries.notes: Domain-specific safety policy

queries.query: IRB approval human subjects research ethics
queries.notes: Domain-specific policy — research ethics

queries.query: courses that use Python programming language
queries.notes: Semantic query — Python mentioned in context of tools, not as a topic keyword

queries.query: what course covers neural networks and deep learning?
queries.notes: Semantic — topic match, not exact course code

queries.query: probability distributions and statistical inference
queries.notes: Semantic match on topic — should retrieve STAT 302

queries.query: competitive advantage business strategy analysis
queries.notes: Semantic paraphrase — 'competitive advantage' not literally in doc, 'competitive analysis' is

queries.query: scientific writing format citations references
queries.notes: Semantic — APA format is the answer but query uses different words

queries.query: signal filtering frequency domain transform
queries.notes: Technical semantic query — frequency domain maps to FFT/DFT topics in ECE 452

queries.query: courses that require a group project
queries.notes: Three documents have group projects — test cross-document recall

queries.query: courses that use R or RStudio for data analysis
queries.notes: Two courses use R — both should appear in top-5

queries.query: courses that require MATLAB
queries.notes: Exact tool name — BM25 finds both

queries.query: which courses have mandatory lab attendance?
queries.notes: Both chem and bio have mandatory lab attendance — cross-document diversity check

queries.query: courses requiring written research paper or report
queries.notes: Semantic — 'written report' paraphrase for research paper

queries.query: courses with participation as graded component
queries.notes: Three courses explicitly grade participation — tests multi-doc cross retrieval

queries.query: which instructor is reachable on LinkedIn?
queries.notes: Very specific detail — only Prof. Wilson mentions LinkedIn

queries.query: course covering how genes are copied and proteins made
queries.notes: Heavy paraphrase — 'genes copied' = DNA replication, 'proteins made' = translation

queries.query: course where missing two lab sessions fails you
queries.notes: Very specific consequence — tests precise policy retrieval

queries.query: can I submit an assignment one week late?
queries.notes: Requires reasoning about time-window policies; multiple docs have different caps

queries.query: randomized experiments and observational study design
queries.notes: Paraphrase — PSYCH 301 covers these under 'Research Methods'

queries.query: which courses have prerequisites in both math and computer science?
queries.notes: Multi-hop: must match courses where prereqs include BOTH CS and MATH subjects
